Intro1: Causal reasoning is the ability to understand how and why things happen, not just whether they occur together. It helps us tell real causes from coincidences, recognize when outcomes have multiple contributing factors, and evaluate what might happen if we intervene or make a change (Pearl, 2009). These skills are important in everyday life. They help us interpret health advice, assess policy claims, and judge whether a product, habit, or decision is actually responsible for an observed result.
Intro2: In the age of AI, causal reasoning faces a particular risk. Many AI systems excel at finding patterns and associations, but they do not explain causal structures. When people rely on AI-generated summaries or predictions without questioning the underlying causal logic, they may become more vulnerable to misleading before-and-after comparisons, spurious correlations, and overconfident policy claims (Hernán & Robins, 2020). Maintaining causal reasoning skills helps users question conclusions, ask the right follow-up questions, and avoid accepting polished explanations that rest on weak causal foundations.
Practice1: Causal reasoning improves with deliberate practice that focuses on thinking about structure rather than doing calculations. Exercises that ask people to identify confounders, consider alternative explanations, separate causes from mechanisms, or reason about counterfactuals build the mental habits needed to evaluate causal claims (Sloman & Lagnado, 2015). Practicing these scenarios helps people recognize common pitfalls and become more skilled at spotting weak arguments. The goal is not to turn users into statisticians. It is to help them think more carefully about “what caused what” in a world full of complex information.
Ref_Hernan: Hernán, M. A., & Robins, J. M. (2020). Causal inference: What if. Boca Raton, FL: Chapman & Hall/CRC.
Ref_Pearl: Pearl, J. (2009). Causality: Models, reasoning, and inference (2nd ed.). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
Ref_Sloman: Sloman, S. A., & Lagnado, D. A. (2015). Causal inference in thinking and decision making. Annual Review of Psychology, 66, 245–267.
